summary: O'Neil shares her personal experience growing up with an abusive father and a mother who stayed with him, using the Ray Rice NFL scandal as a catalyst to process her own trauma. She describes witnessing violence from age 4, being asked at 13 whether her mother should leave, and enduring years of her father's revisionist attempts to gain forgiveness. The public condemnation of domestic violence by NFL commentators gave her permission to accept that some acts are unforgivable. She publishes this to show that domestic violence happens everywhere and to advocate for stronger laws that don't rely on victims pressing charges.
key_points: The economics of leaving an abuser are rarely discussed—managing two households on one salary while already struggling makes leaving seem impossible
Being asked as a child to make adult decisions about family safety destroys childhood and forces premature responsibility
Abusers often engage in revisionist history and guilt-transfer, making their victims' refusal to forgive into the victim's moral failing
Public discourse labeling abuse as 'unforgivable' can liberate survivors from internalized pressure to forgive
Breaking the cycle of violence is a conscious choice and a legitimate point of pride for survivors
money_quotes: When you are a 13-year-old girl who has decided to stand up for your mother and brother against a large and very strong man, who often becomes an enraged and unreasonable bully, you forget about fear and intimidation, because it's just something you cannot think about.

It was my last day of childhood, the day I realized there were no responsible people in my family, and that I would have to step up and be the person who negotiated reasonable boundaries or, failing that, call the cops.
It is unforgivable, but until now I hadn't realized that I was allowed to think so.
He made it my problem for being so unkind as to not forgive him.
Human beings are really good at conflicting emotions.
I've been feeling so guilty for so long at not being able to forgive my father, I never realized that I could just be okay with it.
My proudest accomplishment is that I have not perpetuated the cycle of violence on my own family.
key_insight: Survivors of domestic violence need not carry guilt for refusing to forgive their abusers—some acts are genuinely unforgivable, and accepting that is part of healing.
